structure.title: Preface
structure.summary: The document "Explanation of Sensor Interfaces" is owned and responsibly managed by AUTOSAR, with document ID 913 and status "published," part of the Adaptive Platform in release R25-11. It has undergone multiple revisions since its initial release in 2018-10-31, with most updates involving no content changes except for adding references to ISO 23150:2021 and updating sections. Legal notices state the material is copyrighted, for automotive use only, and prohibits commercial exploitation without a

structure.title: A key point to meet the challenges of developing automated driving functions, especially SAE J3016 automation level 3-5, is standardization: There is a huge potential in reducing development and validation costs by standardizing subsections of the complex hardware and software setup for automated driving functions. Sensors for detecting the vehicle environment and its connected environmental models are considered major enablers for automated driving. The current focus is on the standardization of sensor interfaces that serve as an input to environmental models or data fusion algorithms which in turn serve as the input for automated driving functions equal or greater than SAE level 3. Specifically, the focus is on interfaces for on-board sensors that can independently perceive their environment for performing safety critical tasks, e.g. camera, radar, lidar and ultrasonic sensors. This includes the actual measurement data as well as dynamically changing sensor performance values such as the currently estimated detection range. Other information sources like Car2X and maps are considered as potential extensions. With the resulting standardized sensor interfaces, OEMs, suppliers, service providers and tool providers can reduce their costs and time for the development and validation of automated driving functions.
structure.summary: Standardization of sensor interfaces is critical for reducing development and validation costs for automated driving functions (SAE levels 3-5), as it enables interoperability across OEMs, suppliers, and tools. Key sensors like cameras, radar, lidar, and ultrasonic systems must provide standardized data, including dynamic performance metrics (e.g., detection range), to support environmental models and safety-critical tasks. The ISO 23150 standard defines mandatory and optional sensor signals, along with semantic and coordinate system specifications, which are integrated into AUTOSAR for logical interface design. Fixed optional signals during design-time are essential to ensure runtime reliability,

nodes.title: Intended audience
nodes.summary: The document explains the motivation, usage, and limitations of the Sensor interface standardization for autonomous driving on the AUTOSAR Adaptive platform. It emphasizes the interface's role as a critical connection between stakeholders in future vehicle architectures and software systems. The intended audience includes users of the AUTOSAR Adaptive platform and professionals involved in sensor-based system development for autonomous driving. The text further outlines distinct roles and their motivations for sensor standardization, organized by key technical aspects.

nodes.title: System Designer
nodes.summary: The System Designer perspective emphasizes the need for sensor standardization to support rapid prototyping. This involves creating a comparable and extendable interface that accommodates different sensor types. The goal is to ensure compatibility and flexibility across diverse sensor implementations. The interface must allow for seamless integration and scalability in system design. These requirements align with the need for efficient development and adaptation in dynamic environments.

nodes.title: System Integrator
nodes.summary: The "System Integrator" section emphasizes the necessity of standardized interface and configuration descriptions for enabling generic, testable software implementations and ensuring interoperability between systems. From the perspective of Integrators and Application Developers, these

nodes.title: Safety and Security Engineer
nodes.summary: The Safety and Security Engineer requires the interface to be compatible with standardized mechanisms. This compatibility ensures alignment with established protocols and frameworks. Validation of the interface must occur during the development phase to identify and mitigate potential vulnerabilities. The process emphasizes adherence to safety and security standards throughout implementation. Compliance with these requirements is critical for ensuring system integrity and operational reliability.

nodes.title: Relation to other standards
nodes.summary: The ISO 23150 standard defines a modular, semantic logical interface for data communication between environmental sensors (e.g., camera, lidar, radar) and the vehicle fusion unit in automated driving systems. It supports diverse sensor technologies and fusion concepts. The Open Simulation Interface (OSI) provides a generic software interface for virtual environmental perception, enabling modular, interchangeable components like sensor models and fusion logic. OSI and the Autosar ADI are designed to complement and support ISO 23150's standardization efforts. The text references a figure illustrating

structure.title: Automated Driving Sensor Use Gases
structure.summary: The section outlines the main use case for automated driving sensor standardization, emphasizing stakeholder perspectives across different stages of the process. Chapter 3.1 provides a detailed description of the primary use case, while chapters 3.2–3.5 elaborate on the specific intentions and scope limitations for various stakeholders. Use case 6 in chapter 3.6 expands the focus to virtual sensor simulation, extending the standardization scope beyond physical sensor applications. The text collectively structures the standardization process by aligning stakeholder needs with technical requirements and simulation extensions.

nodes.title: Use Case 2: “Standardized Sensor API”
nodes.summary: The "Standardized Sensor API" use case aims to enable OEMs to define sensor requirements for automated driving using ISO-standardized sensor information and an AUTOSAR-compliant interface, ensuring interoperability between heterogeneous sensors (e.g., LIDAR, RADAR, Camera) and computing units. The scope focuses on standardizing the data format between smart sensors and fusion units, with constraints emphasizing ISO-defined signal interpretation, reusable ISO descriptions, and dependency on ARXML for interface implementation. The interface may vary per sensor implementation but must support common APIs to integrate diverse sensor technologies. Sensor

structure.title: Scope of Sensor Interface Standardization
structure.summary: The sensor interface standardization builds on the ISO specification by expanding it to fully comply with the AUTOSAR Adaptive platform, encompassing syntactic implementation details and additional functionalities like sensor configuration. It excludes mechanical/electrical interfaces and raw data level interfaces, with raw data handling managed via the SWS Communication Management API. The specification assumes sensors connect via a service interface to the Adaptive computing unit, with examples showing direct sensor-ECU connections and data sharing between Adaptive ECUs. The scope intentionally avoids constraints by omitting low-level hardware specifics and focusing on higher-level interface semantics and integration. Figures illustrate sensor setups and inter-ECU data flow scenarios as part of the standardization scope.

structure.title: Sensor Interface API Design
structure.summary: The section titled "Sensor Interface API Design" describes the rationale behind the current API design proposal for Smart Sensors. It focuses on justifying the architectural choices and functional specifications of the interface. The chapter aims to explain the design decisions that align with the requirements of Smart Sensor integration. It does not delve into implementation details but provides context for the proposed API structure. The summary emphasizes the purpose of the chapter as a foundational explanation for the design.

nodes.title: Sensor Interface realization as a Service
nodes.summary: The Sensor Interface is being realized as a Service to universally provide Sensor functionality across AUTOSAR platforms, including both Classic and Adaptive variants. This approach supports future sensor architectures depicted in the referenced figure. The decision ensures compatibility with diverse vehicle deployments and enables seamless integration of Sensor services. A critical note clarifies that the figure does not illustrate a valid scenario for Classic platforms executing ADAS/AD applications.

nodes.title: ISO mapping to Sensor Services
nodes.summary: The ISO standard defines Smart Sensors with object-level lists for tracking, roadmark, and landmark services, which are technology-agnostic. A separate sensor-technology-dependent feature list is used for detection. The Sensor Service design directly maps these ISO structures, resulting in each Smart Sensor providing tracking, roadmark, landmark services, and a detection service tailored to its technology. The figure illustrates this ISO list-to-Sensor service mapping.

nodes.title: Sensor Service Template
nodes.summary: The Sensor Service Template addresses ISO's optional elements by distinguishing between mandatory and optional components, with optional elements predefined at design time by providers and users. A capability vector indicates which optional elements a sensor provides, while varying performance levels (e.g., Low/High profiles) allow different data resolution settings for the same sensor type. Service compatibility is verified during deployment or

nodes.title: Interface stability
nodes.summary: The section highlights that the availability of affordable, suitable sensors is a key driver for autonomous driving, necessitating extensive research and development. The rapid evolution of sensors, including improvements in accuracy and sampling rates, complicates the creation of stable, long-lasting interfaces. These technological advancements introduce variability, making it challenging to define interfaces that remain consistent over time. Interface stability is thus a critical challenge due to the dynamic nature of sensor development. The text emphasizes that ongoing innovation in sensor technology directly impacts the difficulty of maintaining interface longevity.

nodes.title: Interface configuration
nodes.summary: The sensor interface configuration occurs at three distinct times: **design time** (static setup with pre-defined connections, requiring software updates), **connection time** (dynamic selection of interface versions during system startup via handshake protocols, enabling flexibility without full re-flashing), and **runtime** (flexible but less deterministic, complicating safety analysis due to variable configurations). Runtime is deemed unsuitable for autonomous systems due to safety complexity, while design time offers proven safety but lacks adaptability. Connection time is recommended to balance flexibility and safety, accommodating future interface changes without system-wide reconfiguration. Examples include supporting new interfaces for sensors, parallel testing of interfaces, and backward compatibility for legacy systems like ultrasonic sensors with multi-beam capabilities.
